---
title: "A Map of the API"
---

This appendix is the orientation guide to AeroSandbox's public API. You will learn how the namespace is organized (so you know where to look for things), where the complete auto-generated reference documentation lives, how to decode the docstring and type-hint conventions used throughout the library, and what the versioning policy promises when you upgrade. It is deliberately short: the full per-function reference is generated straight from the source code and lives online, so this chapter teaches you how to *navigate* that documentation rather than duplicating it.

## One import, three namespaces {#sec-api-reference-namespaces}

Nearly everything you use in day-to-day AeroSandbox work is reachable through three imports:

```python
import aerosandbox as asb             # all major classes: Opti, Airplane, AeroBuildup, ...
import aerosandbox.numpy as np        # drop-in NumPy that also accepts optimization variables
import aerosandbox.tools.units as u   # unit-conversion factors: u.knot, u.lbm, u.foot, ...
```

The `asb` namespace is intentionally flat: every major class is exported at the top level, so code reads as `asb.Wing`, not `aerosandbox.geometry.wing.Wing`. The table below lists *every* top-level export, grouped by discipline — it is generated live by introspecting the installed library at render time, so it cannot go stale.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
import aerosandbox as asb
import inspect

# Group every top-level export by the subpackage that defines it.
exports: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
for name in dir(asb):
    if name.startswith("_"):
        continue
    obj = getattr(asb, name)
    if not (inspect.isclass(obj) or inspect.isfunction(obj)):
        continue
    module = inspect.getmodule(obj)
    if module is None or not module.__name__.startswith("aerosandbox"):
        continue
    subpackage = module.__name__.split(".")[1] if "." in module.__name__ else "aerosandbox"
    exports.setdefault(subpackage, []).append(name)

n_exports = sum(len(v) for v in exports.values())

GROUP_LABELS = [
    ("optimization", "Optimization"),
    ("geometry", "Geometry"),
    ("aerodynamics", "Aerodynamics analyses"),
    ("atmosphere", "Atmosphere"),
    ("performance", "Operating point"),
    ("dynamics", "Flight dynamics"),
    ("weights", "Mass properties"),
    ("modeling", "Surrogate modeling"),
    ("common", "Framework base classes"),
    ("aerosandbox", "Utilities"),
]
labeled = {key for key, _ in GROUP_LABELS}
rows = [(label, exports[key]) for key, label in GROUP_LABELS if key in exports]
rows += [(key, names) for key, names in sorted(exports.items()) if key not in labeled]

print("| Discipline | Top-level exports (`asb.<name>`) |")
print("|:---|:---|")
for label, names in rows:
    print(f"| {label} | " + ", ".join(f"`{n}`" for n in sorted(names)) + " |")
print()
print(
    f": All {n_exports} top-level exports of the `aerosandbox` namespace, "
    f"generated by introspecting version {asb.__version__} at render time. "
    "{#tbl-api-reference-exports}"
)
```

The other two imports are conventions you will see in every chapter of this book. `aerosandbox.numpy` re-exports all of NumPy, with trace-breaking functions rewritten so they work on both plain arrays and optimization variables ([Optimization Basics](01-optimization-basics.qmd) explains why). `aerosandbox.tools.units` is a module of plain floats — multiplicative conversion factors *to* base SI units — so `airspeed = 100 * u.knot` gives meters per second.

## The subpackage map {#sec-api-reference-map}

Anything not in @tbl-api-reference-exports is imported from its subpackage (e.g., `from aerosandbox.library import propulsion_turbofan`). There are fourteen subpackages; the two under "core" are the ones the library's own documentation says get you 90% of the way there.

| Subpackage | What lives there | Covered in |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `optimization` | **Core.** The `Opti` stack: an object-oriented way to formulate and solve engineering optimization problems. | [Optimization Basics](01-optimization-basics.qmd) |
| `numpy` | **Core.** The drop-in NumPy replacement that preserves the automatic-differentiation trace. | [Optimization Basics](01-optimization-basics.qmd) |
| `geometry` | Object-oriented aircraft geometry — `Airplane`, `Wing`, `Fuselage`, `Airfoil`, and friends — plus 3D drawing and CAD export. | [Geometry](04-geometry.qmd) |
| `aerodynamics` | 2D and 3D aerodynamics analyses: `AeroBuildup`, `VortexLatticeMethod`, lifting-line methods, and wrappers for XFoil, AVL, and MSES. | [Airfoil](05-airfoil-aerodynamics.qmd) & [Aircraft Aerodynamics](06-aircraft-aerodynamics.qmd), [External Tools](17-external-tools.qmd) |
| `atmosphere` | Standard-atmosphere models: the ISA, and a differentiable fit to it. | [Atmosphere, Propulsion & Weights](07-atmosphere-propulsion-weights.qmd) |
| `performance` | `OperatingPoint`: the flight condition (atmosphere, speed, `alpha`, `beta`, rotation rates) an airplane is analyzed at. | [Atmosphere, Propulsion & Weights](07-atmosphere-propulsion-weights.qmd) |
| `dynamics` | The flight-dynamics engine: point-mass and rigid-body models for simulating and optimizing trajectories. | [The Dynamics Stack](12-dynamics-stack.qmd) |
| `structures` | Beam models (`tube_spar_bending`) and buckling criteria (`buckling`). | [Structures](08-structures.qmd) |
| `weights` | `MassProperties` (mass, CG, inertia tensor, with an algebra for combining them) and mass properties of simple shapes. | [Atmosphere, Propulsion & Weights](07-atmosphere-propulsion-weights.qmd) |
| `propulsion` | A placeholder, currently work-in-progress; today's propulsion models live in `library`. | — |
| `library` | Ready-made surrogate models by the hundred: propulsion (`propulsion_electric`, `propulsion_turbofan`, ...), power systems (`power_solar`, ...), structural mass, aerodynamics, costs, winds, field lengths. Some physics-based, some empirical fits; each documents its units and sources in its docstring. | [Atmosphere, Propulsion & Weights](07-atmosphere-propulsion-weights.qmd), [Solar UAV Sizing](15-solar-uav-sizing.qmd) |
| `modeling` | Build-your-own surrogates: `FittedModel`, `InterpolatedModel`, `black_box`. | [Surrogate Modeling](16-surrogate-modeling.qmd) |
| `tools` | Standalone utilities: `units`, `pretty_plots`, string formatting, benchmarking. | used throughout |
| `visualization` | Plotting machinery behind the `.draw()` methods. | — |

: The AeroSandbox subpackage map. {#tbl-api-reference-subpackages}

To give you a quantitative feel for where the library's surface area actually is, @fig-api-reference-surface counts the public classes and functions defined in each subpackage — again computed live against the installed version.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-api-reference-surface
#| fig-cap: "Public API surface area by subpackage, counted by introspection at render time. The largest subpackage by far is the `library` of ready-made surrogate models; the core `optimization` interface you must actually learn is just two classes."
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal bar chart of the number of public classes and functions in each AeroSandbox subpackage. The library subpackage is largest, followed by numpy and tools; the optimization subpackage is smallest with two symbols, annotated as 'the whole core interface: Opti and OptiSol'."
import _common
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import importlib
import pkgutil
import warnings

SKIP = ("test_", ".ignore", ".legacy", "_derivations", "datasets")


def count_public_symbols(subpackage: str) -> int:
    """Count public classes + functions defined within an aerosandbox subpackage."""
    pkg = importlib.import_module(f"aerosandbox.{subpackage}")
    modules = [pkg]
    if hasattr(pkg, "__path__"):
        for info in pkgutil.walk_packages(pkg.__path__, prefix=pkg.__name__ + "."):
            if any(s in info.name for s in SKIP):
                continue
            try:
                with warnings.catch_warnings():
                    warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
                    modules.append(importlib.import_module(info.name))
            except Exception:
                pass  # optional dependency not installed; skip
    seen = set()
    for module in modules:
        for name, obj in vars(module).items():
            if name.startswith("_") or not (inspect.isclass(obj) or inspect.isfunction(obj)):
                continue
            defining_module = inspect.getmodule(obj)
            if defining_module is not None and defining_module.__name__.startswith(pkg.__name__):
                seen.add((defining_module.__name__, name))
    return len(seen)


CORE = {"optimization", "numpy"}
counts = {
    subpackage: count_public_symbols(subpackage)
    for subpackage in [
        "optimization", "numpy", "geometry", "aerodynamics", "atmosphere",
        "performance", "dynamics", "structures", "weights", "library",
        "modeling", "tools", "visualization",
    ]
}
names, values = zip(*sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1]))

# Walking aerosandbox.tools imports its plotting theme, which clobbers the
# book's matplotlib house style -- re-apply it before drawing.
importlib.reload(_common)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.8))
colors = [_common.SERIES[0] if n in CORE else "#b5b5b5" for n in names]
bars = ax.barh(names, values, color=colors)
for bar, name, value in zip(bars, names, values):
    if name == "optimization":
        continue  # this bar gets its own callout below
    ax.text(
        value + max(values) * 0.01, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height() / 2,
        f"{value}", va="center", fontsize=8, color=_common.TEXT_SECONDARY,
    )
ax.annotate(
    f"the whole core interface:\n{counts['optimization']} classes (Opti, OptiSol)",
    xy=(counts["optimization"] + 0.01 * max(values), names.index("optimization")),
    xytext=(0.22 * max(values), names.index("optimization") - 0.6),
    color=_common.SERIES[0], fontweight="bold", fontsize=9, va="center",
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=_common.SERIES[0], lw=1),
)
ax.set_xlabel("Public classes + functions [count]")
ax.set_title("Where the API's surface area lives")
ax.grid(axis="y", visible=False)
plt.show()
```

The shape of this chart is the design philosophy of the library in one picture: a tiny core you must learn (`optimization` is literally two classes — highlighted in blue, along with the `numpy` shim that makes your models differentiable), surrounded by a large, *optional* collection of physics models and utilities you dip into as needed.

## Where the full reference lives {#sec-api-reference-full-docs}

Three places, in decreasing order of formality:

1. **The reference website: [aerosandbox.readthedocs.io](https://aerosandbox.readthedocs.io/).** Complete API documentation for every public module, class, and function, auto-generated from the source docstrings (via `sphinx-autoapi`) — so it is always in sync with the code. This is the place to browse signatures and options for a class you already know you need.
2. **`help()` in your Python session.** Every public object is documented in-source, so `help(asb.Airplane)` or `help(asb.Opti.variable)` prints the same documentation without leaving your terminal. There is also `asb.docs()`, which opens the project website in your browser.
3. **The source itself: [github.com/peterdsharpe/AeroSandbox](https://github.com/peterdsharpe/AeroSandbox).** AeroSandbox is documented extensively in-source, and each subpackage ships its unit tests alongside the code (the `test_*` folders) — hundreds of small, runnable usage examples. When you wonder "how am I *supposed* to call this?", the tests are often the fastest answer.

## Reading docstrings and type hints {#sec-api-reference-conventions}

**Docstrings state the units of every physical input and output.** The library-wide default is base SI units (m, kg, s, N, Pa, W, J), with one long-standing aerospace exception: angle of attack `alpha` and sideslip `beta` are in degrees (all other angles are radians). Here is a representative docstring — note that every physical argument says what unit it expects:

```{python}
import aerosandbox as asb
import inspect

print(inspect.getdoc(asb.Atmosphere.__init__))  # or just: help(asb.Atmosphere)
```

**Type hints tell you what a parameter accepts — including whether it can be symbolic.** Look at the hint on that same `altitude` parameter:

```{python}
print(inspect.signature(asb.Atmosphere.__init__).parameters["altitude"])
```

That union looks intimidating, but it decodes to exactly two facts. Everything up through `numpy.ndarray` says the parameter is *vectorizable*: pass a scalar for one flight condition or an array for many. The `casadi.MX | casadi.DM` tail says the parameter is *hybrid*: it also accepts an optimization variable, meaning you can put this model inside an `asb.Opti` problem and differentiate through it. All three modes, live:

```{python}
import aerosandbox.numpy as np

print(asb.Atmosphere(altitude=11e3).pressure())  # scalar in, scalar out [Pa]

print(asb.Atmosphere(altitude=np.array([0, 10e3, 20e3])).pressure())  # vectorized [Pa]

opti = asb.Opti()
altitude = opti.variable(init_guess=10e3)
pressure = asb.Atmosphere(altitude=altitude).pressure()
print(type(pressure))  # symbolic: an expression the optimizer can differentiate
```

These recurring unions have canonical names, defined (with a design-philosophy writeup worth reading) in `aerosandbox.numpy.typing`: `Scalar`, `Array`, `Vectorizable`, and `ArrayLike` are the hybrid types that accept optimization variables, while their `Concrete*` counterparts (`ConcreteScalar`, `ConcreteArray`, ...) are NumPy-only. When you see a `Concrete*` hint — on file I/O, plotting, or external-tool wrappers like `asb.XFoil` — the function needs actual numbers and *cannot* sit inside an optimization trace ([External Tools](17-external-tools.qmd) shows how to bridge that gap).

## Versioning and deprecation policy {#sec-api-reference-versioning}

AeroSandbox makes a good-faith effort to follow [semantic versioning](https://semver.org/), and its [changelog](https://github.com/peterdsharpe/AeroSandbox/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md) catalogs every change that practical users might care about. The policy, condensed:

* **Significant breaking changes only occur at major version bumps** (e.g., 3.x.x → 4.x.x). An extremely minor breaking change may ship with a minor bump (4.0.x → 4.1.x); every effort is made to keep breaking changes out of patch releases.
* **Deprecation before removal:** for at least one version before a breaking change, the old API keeps working but emits a deprecation warning telling you what to migrate to.
* **Keyword arguments are your stability contract.** Adding new keyword arguments — or reordering existing ones — is *not* considered a breaking change. So call AeroSandbox functions with keyword arguments (`my_function(a=1, b=2)`, not `my_function(1, 2)`); it is more readable anyway, and it immunizes your code against signature evolution.
* **If you depend on AeroSandbox industrially:** pin your version, write a few regression tests around the results you care about, and run them after upgrading. The library also ships its own full test suite, runnable locally in one line: `asb.run_tests()`. If an upgrade breaks something without a prior deprecation warning, that is a bug — please [open an issue](https://github.com/peterdsharpe/AeroSandbox/issues).

This book was rendered against AeroSandbox `{python} asb.__version__`, and every code cell in it executed against that version — so the book itself is one big compatibility test.

## Where to go next {#sec-api-reference-next}

* If a solve is failing and you came here looking for answers, what you want is the [FAQ & Troubleshooting](91-faq-troubleshooting.qmd) appendix.
* If the two-class core (`Opti`, `OptiSol`) is still unfamiliar, [Optimization Basics](01-optimization-basics.qmd) is the chapter everything else builds on.
* For exhaustive signatures and options on any object mentioned here, go straight to [aerosandbox.readthedocs.io](https://aerosandbox.readthedocs.io/).
